data.title: ISM Manufacturing PMI flatlines in April, missing consensus
data.description:
April U.S. ISM Manufacturing PMI: 52.7 vs. 53.0 consensus and 52.7 prior, according to data released by the Institute for Supply Management on Friday.

Manufacturing prices:  84.6 vs. 78.3 in March.

Employment: 46.4 vs. 48.7 prior.

New orders: 54.1 vs. 53.5 prior.

Supplier deliveries: 60.6  vs. 58.9 prior.

Developing… Check back for updates.

data.title: Salesforce goes all-in on AI as FY27 revenue model gets reset: Full details here
data.description:
• Salesforce (CRM) released an investor presentation on Friday stating that it will change its revenue reporting structure from FY27 to better reflect its AI-focused “Agentic Enterprise” strategy.
  • The new structure will mainly split revenue into two buckets: “Agentforce Apps” and “Data 360, Platform & Other."
  • The company will group products like Sales, Slack, MuleSoft, and Tableau under broader AI-focused categories while separately reporting major acquisition revenue like Informatica for one year after closing.
  • As per the presentation, recast FY26 data showed “Agentforce Apps” revenue grew 7% to about $26.7B, while “Data 360, Platform & Other” grew faster at 15% to nearly $12.7B.
  • Total subscription and support revenue rose 10% to around $39.4B in FY26.
  • It will also provide both old and new reporting formats during FY27 for easier comparison before fully switching to the new AI-focused structure in FY28.
  • Following the restructuring news, the shares jumped ~3.34% to ~$182.42.

data.title: Summit Therapeutics falls after update on Akeso-partnered lung cancer trial
data.description:
Summit Therapeutics (SMMT) lost ~22% on Friday after the company said that a late-stage trial for its lead asset, ivonescimab, developed with Chinese biotech Akeso (AKESF), will continue as planned following an uneventful interim analysis.

After an early analysis of its progression-free survival data, the Independent Data Monitoring Committee of its HARMONi-3 trial recommended that the study be continued as planned, the Florida-based biotech said with its Q1 2026 results on Thursday.

The Phase 3 global trial was designed to evaluate ivonescimab in combination with chemotherapy as a first-line option for metastatic non-small cell lung cancer. Its early PFS analysis was expected to support regulatory discussions ahead of the final PFS readout scheduled for H2 2026.

Wall Street analysts had mixed reactions to the update. “The interim PFS analysis was an important potential catalyst that investors were looking forward to and the optics of the new guidance pressured shares," Barclays analyst Etzer Darout wrote with an Equal Weight rating and an $18 target on SMMT.

“There is no change to the timing of the pre-planned final PFS analysis in H2:26," TD Cowen’s Tyler Van Buren added with a Buy rating, according to Bloomberg.

“Furthermore, we anticipate no change to this trial’s probability of success, especially given the minimal alpha spent on the interim analysis," Van Buren argued.

“Summit surprisingly missed interim PFS in major global trial HARMONi-3," Jefferies analyst Faisal Khurshid wrote of trimming his price target on Summit (SMMT) to $17 from $18 per share.

data.title: TMC rises as NOAA says deep seabed mining application in full compliance
data.description:
TMC the metals company (TMC) up 5.3% in early trading Friday after saying the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration determined the company's application for an exploration license and commercial recovery permit is in full compliance with the Deep Seabed Hard Mineral Resources Act.

The consolidated application now moves into the certification stage of the review process, followed by a draft environmental impact statement for the TMC (TMC) project that will be published for public comment; after the EIS is finalized, the NOAA will make a final determination on whether to issue the license and permit, a process the company expects to conclude before the end of Q1 2027.

TMC's (TMC) consolidated application covers a ~65K sq km exploration and commercial recovery area in the Clarion Clipperton Zone in the Pacific Ocean, up from a commercial recovery area of ~25K sq km in the company’s initial application from April 2025; the area contains an estimated resource of 619M metric tons of wet nodules and potential exploration upside of an additional 200M tons.

data.title: Google, Nvidia among 7 inking deals with Pentagon for AI use on classified work
data.description:
The U.S. Department of War has entered into agreements with seven technology companies to use their AI capabilities in classified settings, boosting the Pentagon's efforts to get access to cutting-edge AI.

The Department of War, also known as the Department of Defense, inked agreements with ChatGPT maker OpenAI (OPENAI); Alphabet’s (GOOG) (GOOGL) Google; Elon Musk’s SpaceX (SPACE), which owns xAI (X.AI), the maker of AI chatbot Grok; Microsoft (MSFT); Amazon's (AMZN) Amazon Web Services; Nvidia (NVDA); and startup Reflection AI to deploy their advanced AI capabilities on the department's classified networks for lawful operational use. 

The DoD said that SpaceX, OpenAI, Google, Nvidia, Reflection, Microsoft, and AWS will provide resources to deploy their capabilities for both Impact Level 6 (IL6) and Impact Level 7 (IL7) environments.

The agency noted that the effort supports its AI Acceleration Strategy by enabling new capabilities across its three core tenets of warfighting, intelligence, and enterprise operations.

GenAI.mil, the department's official AI platform, is already demonstrating the scale and impact of this acceleration. Over 1.3M department personnel have used the platform, according to DoD.

The Department of War declined to comment further but directed Seeking Alpha to a post by Emil Michael, the Undersecretary of War for Research and Engineering and Chief Technology Officer, on X. "Today, the @DeptofWar entered into agreements with SEVEN of the world's leading frontier AI model and infrastructure companies to deploy frontier capabilities on the Department's classified networks."

"This is just the latest initiative in our mandate to create an AI-FIRST WAR DEPARTMENT," Michael added.

"For more than a decade, AWS has been committed to supporting our nation's military and ensuring that our warfighters and defense partners have access to the best technology at the best value. We look forward to continuing to support the Department of War's modernization efforts, building AI solutions that help them accomplish their critical missions," said AWS spokesperson Tim Barrett in an email to Seeking Alpha.

Google declined to comment beyond the Department's press release in an email to Seeking Alpha.

SpaceX, OpenAI, Nvidia, Reflection, and Microsoft did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

Michael, speaking to CNBC, said that the Department will look at how to work with all the models in the coming year. He added that they cannot rely on only one partner for AI.

The deals show how much of Silicon Valley is agreeing to the DoD's terms in a way that Anthropic (ANTHRO) did not. The Claude AI chatbot maker — which is backed by Amazon (AMZN) and Google — has been in a legal tussle with the Pentagon. Anthropic had sued the DoD after the department dropped its contract with the company and labeled it a supply chain risk. The company had rejected the Department of War's demand for unrestricted access to its AI models.

However, recently, after Anthropic unveiled its most powerful model, Claude Mythos Preview, a general-purpose frontier model, which it called its "most capable yet for coding and agentic tasks," the Trump Administration seemed to have warmed up a bit. However, the tussle continues.

Michael also told CNBC that Mythos is a separate issue, adding that Mythos is being dealt with government-wide.

Some companies, including OpenAI and SpaceX, had had initial agreements with the Department of War. Earlier this week, it was reported that Google signed a deal allowing the Pentagon to use Google’s AI models on classified work.

Michael said in a statement to the Wall Street Journal that, “We are equipping the warfighter with a suite of AI tools to maintain an unfair advantage and achieve absolute decision superiority."

For many months, Anthropic’s Claude models were some of the only tools available in classified settings through Palantir Technologies (PLTR), which offers AI services to the military through its platform Maven, the report added.

Update: The story was updated with the Department of War's response.

data.title: Studio City International GAAP EPS of $0.01, revenue of $176.7M
data.description:
• Studio City International press release (MSC): Q1 GAAP EPS of $0.01.
  • Revenue of $176.7M.

  • Total gaming taxes and the costs incurred in connection with the on-going operation of the Studio City Casino deducted from gross gaming revenues were US$286.5 million and US$260.2 million in the first quarters of 2026 and 2025, respectively.

    Total non-gaming revenues at Studio City for the first quarter of 2026 were US$89.8 million, compared with US$85.8 million for the first quarter of 2025.

    Operating income for the first quarter of 2026 was US$28.0 million, compared with US$15.3 million in the first quarter of 2025.

    Studio City’s Adjusted EBITDA(1) was US$80.0 million in the first quarter of 2026, compared with US$69.9 million in the first quarter of 2025. The change was mainly attributable to higher revenue from casino contract and non-gaming revenues, partially offset by higher operating costs.

data.title: Kashkari explains his dissent at FOMC meeting over 'easing bias'
data.description:
The Federal Open Market Committee should have indicated that its next rate cut could be either a cut or a hike, Minneapolis Fed President Neel Kashkari said in an essay defending his dissent of the Federal Reserve's statement on Wednesday.

He said he supported the FOMC's decision to hold the federal funds rate at the meeting. But, given the uncertainty over the Iran war's duration, he didn't think it appropriate for the committee's statement to retain the phrase "In considering the extent and timing of additional adjustments to the target range for the federal funds rate…"

A total of four FOMC members dissented at the meeting, the most since 1992. Philadelphia Fed President Anna Paulson and Dallas Fed President Lorie Logan also dissented against the easing bias in the statement, while Fed Governor Stephen Miran dissented in favor of a 25-basis-point cut.

The use of the words “additional adjustments” signals that the next move would be a cut, Kashkari said, since the previous action was a 25-basis-point reduction.

The Iran war's effect on inflation has him concerned. Kashkari looked at two scenarios on how the Iran war could play out. In the first, a "fairly quick reopening" of the Strait of Hormuz would result in core inflation at  ~3% this year. In that event, he envisions an economy where the optimal monetary policy would be to hold rates and then gradually ease over an extended period once the war's inflation shock fades.

The second scenario involves a longer conflict leading to an extended closure of the Strait of Hormuz. That would result in a much larger price shock, increasing inflation and unemployment in the U.S. If long-term inflation expectations become unmoored, the Fed would have to take a strong policy response to bring them down, which could require a series of rate increases, which could weaken the labor market further.

"Given the uncertainty about the path of the conflict and the resulting effects on inflation, employment, and economic growth, I believe the FOMC should offer a policy outlook that signals that the next rate change could be either a cut or a hike, depending on how the economy evolves,"  he said. "This could tighten financial conditions somewhat today, pushing back against a high-inflation scenario that could require an even stronger monetary policy response in the future."

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Ryan Specialty forecasts mid-single-digit 2026 organic growth and 100-150 bps adjusted EBITDAC margin decline amid property rate pressure
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ryan Specialty Holdings, Inc. (RYAN) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "For the quarter, total revenue grew 15%, driven by organic revenue growth of 11.8% and contributions from M&A" (Founder & Executive Chairman Patrick Ryan).
  • "We also repurchased $40 million of our stock" (Founder & Executive Chairman Patrick Ryan).
  • "We continue to operate in one of the most volatile and reactive insurance markets I've ever witnessed" and "we now expect more tempered growth in 2026" (Founder & Executive Chairman Ryan).
  • "We have announced a onetime option grant program in the second quarter, funded entirely by a portion of my own holdings" and "It is structured to be neutral to the company's outstanding share count" (Founder & Executive Chairman Ryan).
  • "Rates continue to decline with large and cat-exposed accounts down 25% to 35%" and "we're anticipating more moderate casualty growth in 2026" (CEO Timothy Turner).
  • "We are making significant and responsible investments in AI leadership and infrastructure" and "AI-enabled and automated submission processing has reduced turnaround times from approximately 24 hours to under 2 hours" (CEO Turner).
  • "In Q1, total revenue grew to $795 million" and "Adjusted EBITDA grew 15.7% to $232 million" (Executive VP & CFO Janice Hamilton).

Outlook

  • "For the full year, we are now guiding to organic revenue growth in the mid-single digits" (Executive VP & CFO Hamilton).
  • "As of today, we are assuming Q2 organic growth to be near 0, with the biggest uncertainty being how property trends play out" (Executive VP & CFO Hamilton).
  • "On margins, we are now guiding to a full year adjusted EBITDAC margin that will be down approximately 100 to 150 basis points year-over-year" and "we are assuming Q2 margins to be in the low 30s" (Executive VP & CFO Hamilton).
  • "The year-over-year decline reflects the revenue impact of the current and evolving market conditions beyond what we described last quarter, the continued absorption of our talent investments, lower fiduciary investment income and higher health care and benefits costs" (Executive VP & CFO Hamilton).

Financial results

  • "In Q1, total revenue grew to $795 million, up 15% period-over-period" and "M&A, which added over 2 percentage points to our top line" (Executive VP & CFO Hamilton).
  • "Adjusted earnings per share grew 20% to $0.47" and "Our adjusted effective tax rate was 26%" (Executive VP & CFO Hamilton).
  • "We ended the quarter at 3.3x total net leverage on a credit basis, well within our 3 to 4x comfort corridor" (Executive VP & CFO Hamilton).
  • "We expect GAAP interest expense net of interest income on our operating funds of approximately $222 million in 2026 with $58 million to be expensed in the second quarter" (Executive VP & CFO Hamilton).
  • "We are making good progress on our Empower program and are on track for a cumulative charge of approximately $160 million through 2028, delivering approximately $80 million of annual run rate savings in 2029" (Executive VP & CFO Hamilton).

Q&A

  • Elyse Greenspan, Wells Fargo: Asked what "mid-single digits" means and how organic growth trends by quarter; Executive VP & CFO Hamilton: "somewhere between the 4% to 6% range" and "we are assuming Q2 organic growth to be near 0".
  • Greenspan, Wells Fargo: Asked how the company balances talent investment amid lower growth; Executive VP & CFO Hamilton: "we are taking thoughtful actions around our cost structure" and cited "Empower" plus "leveraging our AI and digital transformation strategy".
  • Taylor Scott, Barclays: Asked medium-term growth spread vs retail brokers and macro volatility; Founder & Executive Chairman Ryan: "in the soft market, pricing is a headwind" but "we built this platform really with that in mind"; CEO Turner: "our construction float is very strong" though "our quotes and our winning RFPs are sitting for a little bit longer".
  • Jian Huang, Morgan Stanley: Asked on inflation and when pricing reflects it; Chief Executive Officer of RSG Underwriting Managers Miles Wuller: "we are taking inflation into account as we price risk in real time".
  • Tracy Benguigui, Wolfe: Asked what is different in the cycle and whether flow is reversing; Founder & Executive Chairman Ryan: "it's surprising how quickly declines have emerged"; CEO Turner: "the flow into the channel is up 8%, and we're outpacing that".
  • Robert Cox, Goldman Sachs: Asked about retailers internalizing wholesale and AI product innovation; CEO Turner: "we are seeing some of that activity, but it's not a lot" and "we have not seen any particular product innovation around" AI.

Sentiment analysis

  • "Q2 organic growth to be near 0" and "adjusted EBITDAC margin... down approximately 100 to 150 basis points" signaled a slightly negative analyst setup around growth and margin durability (Executive VP & CFO Hamilton).
  • "we remain very bullish on our construction pipeline" and "we're confident that we'll get even more market share this year" reflected slightly positive management tone on demand/positioning despite pricing pressure (CEO Turner).
  • Versus last quarter, guidance language shifted from "high single digits" (Executive VP & CFO Hamilton, Q4 2025) to "mid-single digits" (Executive VP & CFO Hamilton, Q1 2026).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance stepped down from "guiding to organic revenue growth in the high single digits for 2026" (Executive VP & CFO Hamilton, Q4 2025) to "guiding to organic revenue growth in the mid-single digits" plus "Q2 organic growth to be near 0" (Executive VP & CFO Hamilton, Q1 2026).
  • Margin outlook shifted from "an adjusted EBITDAC margin of flat to moderately down" (Executive VP & CFO Hamilton, Q4 2025) to "down approximately 100 to 150 basis points" (Executive VP & CFO Hamilton, Q1 2026).
  • The market narrative remained consistent, with "one of the most volatile and reactive insurance markets" (Founder & Executive Chairman Ryan, Q4 2025) reiterated as "one of the most volatile and reactive insurance markets I've ever witnessed" (Founder & Executive Chairman Ryan, Q1 2026).

Risks and concerns

  • "Rates continue to decline with large and cat-exposed accounts down 25% to 35%" and "competition intensified broadly" (CEO Turner).
  • "guidance embeds continued property rate declines of 25% to 35%" and "a meaningful decline in our property book for the full year" (Executive VP & CFO Hamilton).
  • "growing competition for small and medium hazard risks" and "new capital being deployed" were cited as a driver of "more moderate" casualty growth assumptions (Executive VP & CFO Hamilton).

Final takeaway

Management said Q1 delivered 11.8% organic revenue growth and $0.47 adjusted EPS, but updated full-year 2026 guidance to mid-single-digit organic growth, with Q2 assumed near 0 organic growth and adjusted EBITDAC margin down about 100 to 150 basis points, tying the reset primarily to property rate declines of 25% to 35% and broader competitive pressures, while highlighting ongoing investments in AI, Empower cost actions, and capital allocation including $40 million of share repurchases and a one-time option grant program funded from Patrick Ryan’s holdings.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Canada manufacturing PMI surges to 53.30 in April, up from 50.00 in March
